In this chapter,... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 7 The federal system thus has dual, 1 There are two or more levels (or, 2 Different tiers of government, 3 The jurisdictions of the respective. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Federalism is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that f e d e r a l i s m 13 Chapter 2 Federalism Overview In the previous chapter, we noted that vertical division of power among different levels of government is one of the major forms of power-sharing in modern democracies.

The text explains that in this chapter, we focus on this form of power- sharing.

The text explains that it is most commonly referred to as federalism.

The text explains that we begin by describing federalism in general terms.

The text explains that the rest of the chapter tries to understand the theory and practice of federalism in India.

The text explains that a discussion of the federal constitutional provisions is followed by an analysis of the policies and politics that has strengthened federalism in practice.

The text explains that towards the end of the chapter, we turn to the local government, a new and third tier of Indian federalism.

The text explains that 14 D e m o c r a t i c P o l i t i c s What is federalism?

The text explains that though only 25 of the world’s 193 countries have federal political systems, their citizens make up 40 per cent of the world’s population.

The text explains that most of the large countries of the world are federations.

The text explains that can you notice an exception to this rule in this map?

The text explains that source: Montreal and Kingston, Handbook of Federal Countries: 2002, McGill-Queen’s University Press, 2002.

The text explains that federal political systems Canada United States of America Mexico PaCiFiC OCean Micronesia Argentina Venezuela atlantiC OCean Brazil St.

The text explains that kitts and Nevis Belgium Switzerland Spain Nigeria Ethiopia Comoros Bosnia and Herzegovina Austria Pakistan Russia India Malaysia Australia indian OCean South Africa PaCiFiC OCean United Arab Emirates Germany Let us get back to the contrast between Belgium and Sri Lanka that we saw in the last chapter.

The text explains that you would recall that one of the key changes made in the Constitution of Belgium was to reduce the power of the Central Government and to give these powers to the regional governments.

The text explains that regional governments existed in Belgium even earlier.

The text explains that but all these powers were given to these governments and could be withdrawn by the Central Government.

The text explains that the change that took place in 1993 was that the regional governments were given constitutional powers that were no longer dependent on the central government.

The text explains that thus, Belgium shifted from a unitary to a federal form of government.

The text explains that sri Lanka continues to be, for all practical purposes, a unitary system where the national government has all the powers.

The text explains that tamil leaders want Sri Lanka to become a federal system.

The text explains that federalism is a system of government in which the power is divided between a central authority and various constituent units of the country.

The text explains that usually, a federation has two levels of government.

The text explains that one is the government for the entire country that is usually responsible for a few subjects of common national interest.

The text explains that the others are governments at the level of provinces or states that look after much of the day- to-day administering of their state.

The text explains that both these levels of governments enjoy their power independent of the other.

The text explains that f e d e r a l i s m 15 7 The federal system thus has dual objectives: to safeguard and promote unity of the country, while at the same time accommodate regional diversity.

The text explains that governments at different levels should agree to some rules of power-sharing.

The text explains that an ideal federal system has both aspects : mutual trust and agreement to live together.

The text explains that the exact balance of power between the central and the state government varies from one federation to another.

The text explains that in this first category of federations, all the constituent States usually have equal power and are strong vis-à-vis the federal government.

The text explains that the second route is where a large country decides to divide its power between the constituent States and the national government.

The text explains that in this second category, the Central Government tends to be more powerful vis-à- vis the States.

The text explains that in this sense, federations are contrasted with unitary governments.

The text explains that under the unitary system, either there is only one level of government or the sub-units are subordinate to the central government.

The text explains that the central government can pass on orders to the provincial or the local government.

The text explains that but in a federal system, the central government cannot order the state government to do something.

The text explains that state government has powers of its own for which it is not answerable to the central government.

The text explains that both these governments are separately answerable to the people.

The text explains that let us look at some of the key features of federalism : 1 There are two or more levels (or tiers) of government.

The text explains that 2 Different tiers of government govern the same citizens, but each tier has its own jurisdiction in specific matters of legislation, taxation and administration.

The text explains that marriages Union List State List Concurrent List

The text explains that 28 D e m o c r a t i c P o l i t i c s 1 2 3 4 (a) D A B C (b) B C D A (c) A C D B (d) C D A B Exercises 10.

The text explains that examine the following pairs that give the level of government in India and the powers of the government at that level to make laws on the subjects mentioned against each.

The text explains that which of the following pairs is not correctly matched?

The text explains that in a federation, the powers of the federal and provincial governments are clearly demarcated.

The text explains that india is a federation because the powers of the Union and State Governments are specified in the Constitution and they have exclusive jurisdiction on their respective subjects.

The text explains that sri Lanka is a federation because the country is divided into provinces.

The text explains that india is no longer a federation because some powers of the States have been devolved to the local government bodies.

The text explains that which of the statements given above are correct?

The text explains that (a) A, B and C (b) A, C and D (c) A and B only (d) B and C only (a) State government State List (b) Central government Union List (c) Central and State governments Concurrent List (d) Local governments Residuary powers

Source: NCERT Class 10 Democratic Politics II, Chapter 2: Federalism

The area may be defined in terms of geographical boundaries or in terms of certain kinds of subjects.
In this sense, federations are contrasted with unitary governments.
